era_context: Early Bronze Age III; Egyptian Old Kingdom Fourth Dynasty in its mature phase; Mesopotamian Early Dynastic Period III with competing city-states; Indus Valley Civilization at its height. This is a period of sophisticated urban civilization in multiple regions, though political structures and ideologies vary dramatically (Egyptian theocratic monarchy vs. Indus Valley apparent egalitarianism).

events.title: Fourth Dynasty Egypt — Continued pyramid construction and centralized administration
events.description: The Fourth Dynasty continues its program of monumental construction and centralized state administration. Whether this year falls during Khafre's reign or that of his predecessor/successor depends on which chronological system is adopted. The Fourth Dynasty represents the peak of Old Kingdom power, with the state capable of mobilizing vast labor forces, organizing long-distance trade, and maintaining a complex bureaucracy. The construction of the Giza pyramid complex (Great Pyramid, Pyramid of Khafre, Pyramid of Menkaure) spans the Fourth Dynasty and represents an enormous investment of resources.
key_figures: Khafre or Menkaure — Pharaoh (depending on chronology)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza and other Old Kingdom sites
Turin King List and Manetho's Aegyptiaca

events.certainty_note: The existence of Fourth Dynasty rule and its characteristics are confirmed; the specific year assignment is uncertain due to chronological disputes.

events.title: Mesopotamian Early Dynastic Period III — City-state competition and warfare
events.description: In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic Period III (c. 2600–2334 BCE) is characterized by competition among city-states (Lagash, Umma, Uruk, Ur, Nippur) for control of irrigation systems, trade routes, and religious authority. Warfare between city-states is documented in later king lists and inscriptions. The period sees the development of increasingly sophisticated administrative systems, including written contracts and accounts. No single unified state exists; instead, a network of competing polities with varying degrees of power.

sources.name: Sumerian King List (later compilation, but preserves traditions of ED III)
Inscriptions from Lagash and other city-states (e.g., Entemena, Eannatum)

events.certainty_note: The existence of Early Dynastic III city-states and their conflicts are well-attested; specific events for this particular year are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for the year 2961 BCE in any surviving source. The events listed are inferred from the broader era context and are not tied to this particular year. The chronological uncertainty noted above applies equally to this year.
historiographic_note: This year, like most years in the third millennium BCE, has no specific documented events. The historical record is constructed from archaeological stratigraphy, king lists compiled centuries later, and inscriptions that rarely provide precise annual dating. The absence of specific events for this year is not evidence that nothing happened; rather, it reflects the limitations of the historical record.
